Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed verbal phrase: direct object wātar plus locative parna ānda and active pīya-; idiom usually finite in 1st–3rd person.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To calm a quarrel or restore peace inside a household or small group.
Usage Contexts: family mediation; daily speech; conflict resolution; advice
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Synthetic Cuneiform Luwian-style expression built from house-and-water imagery common to ritual purification and domestic order in Hattusa tablet culture.
First Example Sentence: mān tatiš annaš karpīyanta, zika wātar parna ānda pīyasi.
Second Example Sentence: išḫaš lapana na zinnati, apas wātar parna ānda pīyati.
Third Example Sentence: anza taniman šarrīyami, ammuk-ma wātar parna ānda pīyawi.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed transitive verbal phrase: accusative tuppin with adverb āppan and active tarna-; often used with contrastive -ma.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To ignore written evidence, advice, or an obvious warning.
Usage Contexts: scribal work; legal warning; workplace; sarcasm
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Modeled on scribal tablet culture, where written records, ritual prescriptions, and instructions carried high authority.
First Example Sentence: DUB.SAR-aš ḫatrāi, apas-ma tuppin āppan tarnati.
Second Example Sentence: zika tuppin āppan tarnasi, kuitman šāra parā īdi.
Third Example Sentence: mān lugal-aš wiyan pīyati, nāwi tuppin āppan tarnanta.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Compact transitive verb idiom: collective object ḫāwanzan with active šarra-; causative reading arises from the object metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To split a community, family, or team into factions.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Pastoral imagery is appropriate for Bronze Age Anatolia, where herds, offerings, and household economies recur in ritual and administrative contexts.
First Example Sentence: kuis ḫāwanzan šarrati, apas parnašši lāman na ēšzi.
Second Example Sentence: zika ḫāwanzan na šarrasi, annaš taniman arḫa tarnai.
Third Example Sentence: tuppi piran ḫāwanzan šarranta, ūwaš parnanza karpīyanti.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Motion-verb idiom: accusative boundary noun irḫan plus directional parā and verb īya-/ī-; metaphorical extension from spatial crossing.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To overstep a boundary, exceed authority, or act beyond one’s proper role.
Usage Contexts: court protocol; workplace; family hierarchy; moral instruction
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Boundary language reflects land, road, and jurisdictional concerns familiar from Anatolian political and administrative discourse.
First Example Sentence: mān šalliš DUMU-aš irḫan parā īdi, tatiš uwai.
Second Example Sentence: DUB.SAR-aš irḫan parā na īdi, apa tuppin ḫandā ḫatrāi.
Third Example Sentence: zika irḫan parā īyasi, ammuk annallan pīyawin.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Mediating verbal phrase: locative/directional ānda with river noun ḫūpparan and active lāḫu-; can be literal or metaphorical.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To clear one’s name, purify a fault, or repair reputation after accusation.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Water and washing imagery is modeled on ritual purification motifs known from Anatolian cuneiform religious texts.
First Example Sentence: apas kuit ḫūwatar ḫūwai, ḫūpparan ānda lāḫuti.
Second Example Sentence: mān zika lāman šarrasi, ḫūpparan ānda lāḫuwi.
Third Example Sentence: malḫašši piran anza ḫūpparan ānda lāḫūmi.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Comparative idiom: divine/logographic comparator DUTU-iwar with perception verb au-; usually finite and evidential in tone.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To see a matter clearly, transparently, and without hidden confusion.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The Sun as a symbol of visibility and judgment is widely shared in Hittite-Luwian religious environments; this is a synthetic Cuneiform Luwian-style formulation.
First Example Sentence: mān tuppiša āntara ēšzi, DUB.SAR-aš DUTU-iwar auti.
Second Example Sentence: zika ammuk taniman pīyasi, ammuk DUTU-iwar auwi.
Third Example Sentence: lugal-aš piran apas ḫandā DUTU-iwar auta.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Ceremonial-object idiom: accusative NINDA-an plus dative/genitive phrase išḫaš piran and active tarna-; politeness may shade into flattery.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To curry favor, flatter a superior, or offer a symbolic gift to gain approval.
Usage Contexts: court speech; workplace politics; diplomacy; satire
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Modeled on offering language and food-gift etiquette in palace and ritual contexts, with NINDA reflecting common cuneiform logographic practice.
First Example Sentence: kuis NINDA-an išḫaš piran tarnati, apas ḫūman lāman zinnati.
Second Example Sentence: zika NINDA-an išḫaš piran na tarnasi, ḫandā uttar pīyasi.
Third Example Sentence: DUMU-aš ḫūittiyanza NINDA-an išḫaš piran tarnata.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Mythicized verbal phrase: divine name/object tarḫuntan with hearth locative ḫašša ānda and motion verb ḫūwa-; conveys escalation.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To bring overwhelming force into a small matter; to escalate a dispute unnecessarily.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Uses the Storm-god Tarḫunt and hearth imagery to contrast cosmic power with domestic scale; synthetic but culturally anchored.
First Example Sentence: mān DUMU-aš malli ḫūwai, tatiš tarḫuntan ḫašša ānda na ḫūwati.
Second Example Sentence: zika ḫūman karpīyasi, tarḫuntan ḫašša ānda ḫūwasi.
Third Example Sentence: annaš apān wiyanti, kuitman apas tarḫuntan ḫašša ānda ḫūwata.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Ritual-object idiom: object kuršamman with preverbial piran and active ḫuiya-; implies public display before a group.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To expose hidden wrongdoing or reveal what someone tried to conceal.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The kurša-/sacred bag motif is associated with Anatolian ritual symbolism; here it is adapted as a metaphor for hidden contents becoming visible.
First Example Sentence: DUB.SAR-aš tuppin ḫatrāi, kuršamman piran ḫuiyati.
Second Example Sentence: zika uttar ḫalluwasi, apas-ma kuršamman piran ḫuiyati.
Third Example Sentence: mān šāra parna ānda ēšzi, anza kuršamman piran ḫuiyami.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Simile-based verbal idiom: nominative/accusative uttar plus genitival comparison išḫāraš iwar and speech verb lalḫa-; sound-symbolic force.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To speak words that wound deeply or create lasting hostility.
Usage Contexts: literature; insult; diplomacy; curse language; conflict analysis
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Blood-and-word imagery echoes oath, curse, and ritual harm concepts in ancient Anatolian discourse; the expression is synthetic and not presented as a quoted tablet idiom.
First Example Sentence: kuis uttar išḫāraš iwar lalḫati, apas ḫāwanzan šarrati.
Second Example Sentence: zika uttar išḫāraš iwar na lalḫasi, mān āššu tarnašši.
Third Example Sentence: lugal-aš piran apas uttar išḫāraš iwar lalḫata.
